case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).
case.notes: Model-matrix benchmark for upstream/council combinations on the DES isoxazole protocol.

batch_record.reaction_description: Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate were added to TBAB/EG DES in an oven-dried vial; the reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C for 15 min, achieving full conversion to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition.
batch_record.raw_text: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).

mechanism_steps.description: Formation of nitrile oxide from ethyl nitroacetate followed by cycloaddition with phenylacetylene

chemistry_plan.mixing_order_reasoning: Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate must be mixed to generate the nitrile oxide in situ.
chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: The mechanism is based on known chemistry of nitrile oxides and 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition.

analogies.record_id: Yang 등 - 2024 - Safety Guided Process Development and Scale-Up of the Highly Energetic Compound 4-Methyl-1,2,5-oxadi.pdf
analogies.summary: This process involves the synthesis of 4-methyl-1,2,5-oxadiazole, a highly energetic compound, using a nitration or cyclization reaction in a concentrated sulfuric acid/water mixture (63:6.3 ratio). The flow process was conducted in a tubular reactor at 154°C with a residence time of 179 minutes (approximately 3 hours) and a flow rate of 0.12 mL/min, requiring a back-pressure regulator at 47.6 bar to maintain liquid phase conditions. Flow chemistry enabled a significant temperature increase from 65°C in batch to 154°C, resulting in an improved yield from 70.8% to 78.8%. The primary advantage of flow over batch was enhanced safety when handling this highly energetic compound, as the continuous process allows for better control of the exothermic reaction and reduces the inventory of hazardous material at any given time. The high pressure and temperature conditions achievable in the flow reactor also contributed to the improved reaction efficiency.

additives.name: N-methyl morpholine
additives.role: forms stable NMM salt of the product

optimization_table.key_findings: Increasing the residence time to 180 min and temperature to 157 °C resulted in complete conversion and >99% product purity.

analogies.summary: This photocycloaddition reaction utilizes triplet sensitization with 2,2'-dimethoxythioxanthone (2,2'-MeOTX) as the photocatalyst at 1 mol% loading under 455 nm COB LED irradiation. The flow process operates in acetonitrile at 200°C with a 5-minute residence time and 6.0 mL/min flow rate using a FlowSyn steel coil reactor (30 mL volume) equipped with a 34 bar back-pressure regulator. Flow chemistry enabled daisy-chaining of the initial photochemical step with high-temperature thermal cascade reactions, dramatically improving yield from 16% in batch (2 hours) to 88% in flow with a 24-fold intensification factor. The high-temperature operation required for the thermal cascade was much safer to implement in the continuous flow reactor compared to batch processing.

chemistry.reaction_name: [2 + 2] photocycloaddition followed by thermal electrocyclic cascade
chemistry.bond_formed: C-C bonds in cyclobutane ring and subsequent cascade products
chemistry.scope_summary: Five aryl enamide derivatives tested (phenyl, 3-F, 3-Br, 3-OMe, 4-CF3) with yields of 63-68% for most substrates. The 4-CF3 derivative was resistant to the thermal fragmentation step.

optimization_table.key_findings: MeCN solvent with 0.5 M concentration at 200°C with 5 min residence time gave optimal results

figures.key_information: photochemical reactor setup with dual light sources positioned above a round bottom flask on a heating plate
figures.caption: Figure 3.  One-pot photo/thermal batch reaction. | Figure 4.  Batch/Flow process and substrate scope tested. Yields in parentheses are NMR yields.

Figure 7 . The ease at which this could be purified by filtration represents one of the main benefits of acetonitrile over xylene, which would not be possible without the use of continuous flow technology. This result represents a 24 h productivity of 490 g. In conclusion, we have demonstrated a rare example of an excited-state photochemical reaction successfully daisy-chained with a thermal r | Figure 6.  Daisy-chained PhotoSyn/FlowSyn and pure product collected by filtration. | Figure 7.  Flow schematic for the optimized fully continuous photo/thermal cascade sequence.

translation_logic.batch_limitation: Low yield (16%) and long reaction time (120 min) in batch mode
translation_logic.flow_advantage: Daisy-chaining photochemistry with high-temperature thermal cascade reactions enabled by flow technology, achieving high productivity
translation_logic.what_changed: Temperature increased to 200°C, residence time reduced to 5 min, back pressure of 34 bar applied
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Solvent (MeCN), concentration (0.5 M), light source power (36 W)
translation_logic.safety_improvement: High temperature thermal cascade reactions safer in flow than batch

figure_descriptions: Reactor scheme showing photochemical setup with light sources
Flow schematic for daisy-chained photo/thermal process

full_record.notes: High confidence extraction with clear quantitative data from optimization tables. Flow process shows dramatic improvement in yield (16% to 88%) and time reduction (120 min to 5 min). Productivity of 20 g/h mentioned in key claim.

analogies.summary: This process involves stilbene ester synthesis via nucleophilic addition-elimination mechanism using sodium methoxide as the base in methanol solvent. The reaction was conducted in a T-shaped borosilicate glass microreactor with electroosmotic flow (EOF) pumping at a flow rate of 0.4 mL/min and 20-minute residence time. The flow process achieved a 70% yield compared to 60% in batch under similar conditions (20-minute reaction time in methanol). The microreactor setup provided improved mixing and heat transfer compared to batch processing, resulting in a 10 percentage point yield improvement. The single-phase liquid operation in the microreactor enabled better control over reaction conditions and more efficient mass transfer.

chemistry.scope_summary: The study demonstrates the synthesis of a series of nitrostilbene esters using micro reactor technology, with varied reaction efficiencies depending on aldehyde substrates. The micro reactors produced yields of up to 70% under optimized conditions and showed potential for automated synthesis and analogue chemistry.

optimization_table.key_findings: Using an optimum pumping voltage of 400 V resulted in a well-controlled flow condition and higher yield.

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Reaction scheme for the coupling of 2-nitrobenzyltriphenylphosphonium bromide and methyl 4-formylbenzoate in a micro reactor under EOF. | Fig. 2 Schematic of the T-shaped manifold used in the reactor for the Wittig synthesis.

figures.key_information: A, B, C indicate inlets or paths
figures.caption: Fig. 1 Reaction scheme for the coupling of 2-nitrobenzyltriphenylphosphonium bromide and methyl 4-formylbenzoate in a micro reactor under EOF. | Fig. 2 Schematic of the T-shaped manifold used in the reactor for the Wittig synthesis.

Fig. 3 Summary of the product yields obtained for both micro reactor and traditional batch methods for four aldehydes based on the optimisation with methyl 4-formylbenzoate using 2 + 1 stoichiometry. The aldehydes shown are methyl 4-formylbenzoate (A), 3-benzyloxybenzaldehyde (B), 2-naph- thaldehyde (C) and 5-nitrothiophene-2-carboxaldehyde (D). | Fig. 4 Photographs obtained from an optical microscope showing the Wittig reaction ylide intermediate formation under variable flow control.

The aldehydes shown are methyl 4-formylbenzoate (A), 3-benzyloxybenzaldehyde (B), 2-naph- thaldehyde (C) and 5-nitrothiophene-2-carboxaldehyde (D). | Fig. 4 Photographs obtained from an optical microscope showing the Wittig reaction ylide intermediate formation under variable flow control.

Fig. 5 Summary of the product yields obtained for both micro reactor and traditional batch methods for four aldehydes based on the optimisation with methyl 4-formylbenzoate using 1 + 1 stoichiometry. The aldehydes shown are methyl 4-formylbenzoate (A), 3-benzyloxybenzaldehyde (B), 2-naph- thaldehyde (C) and 5-nitrothiophene-2-carboxaldehyde (D).

full_record.notes: Reactor scheme figure confirms 'number_of_inlets' as 3 and presence of BPR as false.

steps.name: Kinetics & Residence Time
steps.summary: τ = 15.0 min, range [1.0–22.5] min, via analogy (IF = 1×)
warnings: Methods disagree: τ_analogy = 15.0 min vs τ_class = 1.5 min (10.0× difference). Experimental verification strongly recommended.

steps.name: Reactor Sizing
steps.summary: V_R = 7.50 mL, d = 1.00 mm, L = 9.55 m, Pe = 173 (✓ plug flow)

steps.name: Mass Transfer
steps.summary: t_mix = 101.3 s, Da = 1.969 — Transition zone — mixing becoming significant
warnings: Da = 1.97 > 1: mixing may limit conversion. Consider static mixer insert.
assumptions: D = 1e-09 m²/s (typical for small organic molecules in liquid)

steps.name: Heat Transfer
steps.summary: Q_gen = 0.0886 W, Q_rem = 90.0000 W, Da_th = 0.0010 — Thermally safe

steps.name: Back-Pressure Regulator
steps.summary: BPR REQUIRED: 5.0 bar (Gas-liquid system detected — BPR mandatory to maintain gas solubility (Henry's law: C_gas = H·P) and prevent uncontrolled degassing.)
equations: C_{\mathrm{gas}} = H \cdot P_{\mathrm{partial}}\quad\text{(higher BPR} \Rightarrow \text{more dissolved gas)}

steps.name: Process Metrics
steps.summary: STY = 0.3320 mol/(L·h), P = 2.490 mmol/h, ṅ_lim = 0.0333 mmol/min, startup waste = 22.5 mL, IF = 1×

streams.pump_role: Deliver reactants for the cycloaddition reaction
streams.reasoning: From Chemistry Plan stream_logic: Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate are the reactants for the cycloaddition.

proposal.mixing_order_reasoning: From Chemistry Plan: Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate must be mixed to generate the nitrile oxide in situ.
post_reactor_steps: Quench with water to stop the reaction and extract product.
proposal.chemistry_notes: The reaction is a 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition requiring thermal activation at 120 °C. The residence time of 15 minutes is based on literature analogies and is within the calculated range. The use of TBAB/EG DES as a solvent is consistent with the batch conditions, ensuring similar reactivity. The flow setup allows for improved heat transfer and reaction control compared to batch processing.

reasoning_per_field.residence_time_min: Analogy 1 used 15 min. Calculation estimated 15 min. Using 15 min because it aligns with literature and ensures sufficient reaction time.
reasoning_per_field.temperature_C: Same as batch — no heat advantage needed as the reaction is already optimized at 120 °C.
reasoning_per_field.BPR_bar: Calculation: BPR required due to gas generation; set at 5 bar to maintain liquid phase conditions.
reasoning_per_field.tubing_material: PFA is suitable for high-temperature applications and is resistant to the DES solvent.
reasoning_per_field.concentration_M: From batch conditions, concentration is set to 0.1 M to match the initial conditions.
